learningGoal: How to put 3 people in order using clues — like solving a mini detective puzzle!
How to use the Think It Through method: read, draw, answer

screens.title: Can you crack the order?
screens.body:
Aisha is working out who is tallest in her group.

Here are your clues:
• Daisy is taller than Evie.
• Evie is taller than Freya.

**Who is the shortest?**

With 3 people, all you need to do is put them in a line from top to bottom. Let's figure it out!

screens.title: Find the link name
screens.body: The secret to ordering is finding the **link name** — the person who appears in BOTH clues. They go in the middle!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Read, draw, answer — the Think It Through method. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always read ALL clues first, then draw, then answer!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Use the 3 steps: **Read all clues → Draw the order → Answer from your diagram**

• Sophie is taller than Liam.
• Liam is taller than Ruby.

interaction.question: Who is the tallest?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Sophie > Liam > Ruby in height. Sophie is at the top of the order, so she is the tallest. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Sophie". Sophie > Liam > Ruby in height. Sophie is at the top of the order, so she is the tallest. ✓

screens.title: 3-person ordering — nailed it!
screens.body: You've cracked the method for ordering 3 people:

learningGoal: How to avoid the sneaky trap of reading clues backwards
Why 'A is taller than B' always means A goes ABOVE B — not the other way round!

screens.title: Is "Holly" really the answer?
screens.body:
Evie is checking her practice test answers.

Clues:
• Ivy is taller than Grace.
• Grace is taller than Holly.

**Who is the tallest?**

Someone answered **"Holly"** — because Holly was mentioned last, so she must be tallest.

But is that right?

screens.title: Read the clue words!
screens.body:
Being mentioned last doesn't mean tallest! You need to read what the clues actually say.

The position a name appears in the sentence does NOT tell you its position in the order. You must read the **clue words** — "taller than", "older than", "more than".

screens.title: Your turn — get it right!
screens.body:
• Lottie is heavier than Max.
• Max is heavier than Ava.

**Who is the lightest?**

interaction.question: Who is the lightest?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Lottie > Max > Ava in weight. Ava is at the bottom, so she is the lightest. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Ava". Lottie > Max > Ava in weight. Ava is at the bottom, so she is the lightest. ✓

screens.title: You won't get caught out now!
screens.body: Now you know the trap, you'll dodge it every time:

learningGoal: How to order 4 people — same method, just one more clue to work with!
How to spot the 'link name' that connects two clues into one chain

screens.title: Can you order all 4?
screens.body:
Aisha is sorting four runners by speed.

Four people means more clues — but the method is the same one you already know! The trick is to find the name that appears in TWO clues. That's your link.

• Hugo is faster than Priya.
• Toby is faster than Hugo.
• Priya is faster than Kai.

**Who came third in the race?**

screens.title: Connect the chains
screens.body: We need to order **Hugo, Priya, Toby, Kai**. With 4 people, you get 3 clues. Look for the **shared name** that links two clues together:

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Put them in order, then answer:

• Mia is taller than Alfie.
• Alfie is taller than Chloe.
• Luca is taller than Mia.

interaction.question: Who is the shortest?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Luca > Mia > Alfie > Chloe. Chloe is at the bottom, so she is the shortest. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Chloe". Luca > Mia > Alfie > Chloe. Chloe is at the bottom, so she is the shortest. ✓

screens.title: 4 people — same skill, bigger chain!
screens.body: Brilliant work! For 4 people, just extend your chain by one more name:

learningGoal: How to stay cool when clues arrive in a jumbled-up order
Why reading ALL clues before you start drawing is the key to getting it right

screens.title: The clues are jumbled — can you still solve it?
screens.body:
Evie is solving a puzzle where the clues come in a tricky order.

Sometimes the clues don't arrive in a helpful order. That's why you must read ALL clues first!

• Kai is shorter than Toby.
• Freya is taller than Toby.
• Amara is shorter than Kai.

**Who is the tallest?**

screens.title: Read ALL clues first!
screens.body: We need to order **Kai, Toby, Freya, Amara** — but the clues come in a tricky order. Don't start drawing after the first clue. Read them all, THEN build the chain:

screens.title: Your turn — a new puzzle!
screens.body:
Read all the clues first, then build the chain:

• Ruby runs faster than Zara.
• Zara runs faster than Isla.
• Oscar runs faster than Ruby.

interaction.question: Who is the slowest?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Oscar > Ruby > Zara > Isla. Isla is at the bottom, so she is the slowest. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Isla". Oscar > Ruby > Zara > Isla. Isla is at the bottom, so she is the slowest. ✓

screens.title: Jumbled clues? No problem!
screens.body: Even when clues come in a confusing order, you know exactly what to do:

learningGoal: How to decide if something is DEFINITELY true, DEFINITELY false, or just MAYBE
Why three little words — 'all', 'some' and 'none' — completely change the answer

screens.title: Is it DEFINITELY true?
screens.body:
Aisha is working through true/false logic puzzles.

These questions are all about spotting one tiny word that changes everything. Is something DEFINITELY true, or only MAYBE true?

• All dogs have four legs.
• Rover is a dog.

**Does Rover have four legs?**

The secret? Watch for these key words: **all**, **some**, **none**.

screens.title: All, Some, or None?
screens.body:
The clues say: All dogs have four legs. Rover is a dog. — watch for the key word!

These three words change EVERYTHING:

• **All** = every single one → definitely true
• **Some** = a few → maybe true, maybe not
• **None** = zero → definitely false

interaction.sentence: The three key words that change the logic are: all, ____, and none
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! "All" = definitely true, "some" = maybe, "none" = definitely false. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — the three key words are all, some, and none!

steps.text: Clues: All dogs have four legs. Rover is a dog.
steps.why: Spot the key word: all, some, or none?

steps.text: Logic: All dogs → four legs. Rover is a dog → Rover has four legs.
steps.why: Draw the logic with arrows

steps.text: Answer: Does Rover have four legs? → Yes, definitely
steps.why: The key word tells you if it's definite or not ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Spot the key word, then decide:

• All cats have whiskers.
• Mittens is a cat.

interaction.question: Does Mittens have whiskers?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! ALL cats have whiskers. Mittens IS a cat. So Mittens DEFINITELY has whiskers. 'All' means every single one! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Yes, definitely". ALL cats have whiskers. Mittens IS a cat. So Mittens DEFINITELY has whiskers. 'All' means every single one! ✓

screens.title: Three words that change everything!
screens.body: Remember these three words and you'll ace every logic question:

steps.text: ALL → definitely true for everyone
steps.why: All dogs have legs → every dog has legs

steps.text: SOME → maybe true, maybe not
steps.why: Some children like maths → you can't be sure about one child

steps.text: NONE → definitely false for everyone
steps.why: No fish can walk → no fish at all can walk ✓

learningGoal: Why 'All X are Y' does NOT mean 'All Y are X' — this trips up loads of people!
How to spot and dodge the backwards logic trap

screens.title: Is "Yes, definitely" really right?
screens.body:
Evie is checking a tricky logic answer.

• All roses have thorns.
• This plant has thorns.

**Is this plant definitely a rose?**

Someone answered **"Yes, definitely"** — because it has thorns and all roses have thorns.

Can you spot the mistake?

screens.title: The backwards logic trap!
screens.body:
"All X are Y" does NOT mean "All Y are X"!

Just because ALL roses have thorns doesn't mean ONLY roses have thorns! Cacti and holly have thorns too.

steps.text: The clue says: All roses have thorns.
steps.why: This tells us about X → Y

steps.text: But we're told: This plant has thorns.
steps.why: We only know about Y, not X

steps.text: The answer is: No, not necessarily
steps.why: Just because ALL roses have thorns doesn't mean ONLY roses have thorns! Cacti and holly have thorns too. ✓

screens.title: Your turn — spot the trap!
screens.body:
• All penguins can swim.
• This animal can swim.

**Is this animal definitely a penguin?**

interaction.question: Is this animal definitely a penguin?
options: Only if it's black and white
Only if it lives in the cold
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! All penguins can swim, but lots of OTHER animals can swim too — fish, seals, dogs. Swimming doesn't prove it's a penguin. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "No, not necessarily". All penguins can swim, but lots of OTHER animals can swim too — fish, seals, dogs. Swimming doesn't prove it's a penguin. ✓

screens.title: The backwards trap — you'll never fall for it!
screens.body: This golden rule will save you marks in the exam:

steps.text: All X are Y ≠ All Y are X
steps.why: All cats have tails, but not all tailed animals are cats

steps.text: If A → B, that doesn't mean B → A
steps.why: Rain makes ground wet, but wet ground doesn't prove rain

steps.text: Ask: could something ELSE cause this?
steps.why: If yes, the answer is 'not necessarily' ✓

learningGoal: How to read comparison words like a pro — taller, shorter, older, younger, more, fewer
Why understanding these words is like having a compass that always points the right way

screens.title: What do the comparison words tell you?
screens.body:
Aisha is learning what comparison words tell you.

**Did you know?** Comparison words are like arrows — they point you to who goes where! Words like "taller than" and "more" point UP. Words like "shorter than" and "fewer" point DOWN.

• Kai is heavier than Toby.
• Freya is lighter than Toby.

**Who is the lightest?**

screens.title: Comparison word decoder
screens.body:
Let's decode the clues: Kai is heavier than Toby. Freya is lighter than Toby.

Each comparison word tells you a direction:

• **More / taller / heavier / older / faster / before** → goes ABOVE
• **Less / shorter / lighter / younger / slower / after** → goes BELOW

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Find the comparison words, decide direction, then draw. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first find the comparison words, then decide up or down, then draw!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Decode the comparison words:

• Sam is shorter than Beth.
• Beth is shorter than Jude.

interaction.question: Who is the tallest?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Shorter than' means goes BELOW. Sam < Beth < Jude, so Jude > Beth > Sam. Jude is the tallest. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Jude". 'Shorter than' means goes BELOW. Sam < Beth < Jude, so Jude > Beth > Sam. Jude is the tallest. ✓

screens.title: Your comparison word cheat sheet!
screens.body: Learn these pairs and you'll never get tripped up:

learningGoal: How to find the tallest, shortest, oldest or youngest — read it straight off your diagram!
Why building the FULL order first means you'll always pick the right answer

screens.title: Who is the tallest?
screens.body:
Aisha is finding the tallest from her ordering diagram.

Superlative questions ask for the **most** or **least** — the person at the very TOP or very BOTTOM of the order.

• Evie is taller than Hugo.
• Hugo is taller than Grace.
• Grace is taller than Ravi.

Build the full order first, THEN look at the ends!

screens.title: Build the order, then check the ends
screens.body: We need to find **who is the tallest** from these clues. Superlative (a word meaning the most or least of something) = the extreme end of the order. Build the FULL chain first:

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Build the full order, then find the answer:

• Seb has more goals than Tilly.
• Tilly has more goals than Noor.
• Noor has more goals than Arlo.

interaction.question: Who has scored the most goals?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Seb > Tilly > Noor > Arlo. Seb is at the very top, so he has scored the most goals. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Seb". Seb > Tilly > Noor > Arlo. Seb is at the very top, so he has scored the most goals. ✓

screens.title: Superlatives — just check the ends!
screens.body: For "who is the tallest / shortest / most / least?" questions, the answer is always at one end of your chain:

learningGoal: How to unscramble jumbled words into a sentence that makes sense
How to spot the starting word and build the sentence piece by piece

screens.title: Can you unscramble the sentence?
screens.body:
Aisha is unscrambling a jumbled sentence.

Jumbled words: 'park the played in children the'

The trick is to find the word that makes sense at the START, then build the sentence piece by piece.

**What is the last word of the correct sentence?**

steps.text: Jumbled words: 'park the played in children the'
Find the starting word, then build outward

screens.title: Start with the subject!
screens.body:
The jumbled words make the sentence: **"The children played in the park."**

To unscramble a sentence:

1. **Find the subject** — who or what is the sentence about?
2. **Find the verb** — what are they doing?
3. **Add the rest** — where, when, how?

interaction.sentence: To unscramble a sentence, first find the ____ — who or what the sentence is about
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! The subject (who/what) usually starts the sentence — find it first. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always find the subject first: who or what is the sentence about?

steps.text: Find the subject (who/what)
steps.why: "The" starts the sentence

steps.text: Find the verb (what happens)
steps.why: Look for the action word — what is the subject doing?

steps.text: Build the sentence: "The children played in the park."
steps.why: What is the last word of the correct sentence? → park ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Jumbled words: 'shop the walked to girls the'

Rearrange the words, then answer:

interaction.question: What is the last word of the correct sentence?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The correct sentence is 'The girls walked to the shop.' The last word is 'shop'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "shop". The correct sentence is 'The girls walked to the shop.' The last word is 'shop'. ✓

screens.title: Sentence unscrambling — sorted!
screens.body: Three steps and you'll crack any jumbled sentence:

steps.text: 1. Find the subject — who or what is it about?
steps.why: This is usually the first word

steps.text: 2. Find the verb — what are they doing?
steps.why: This usually comes second or third

steps.text: 3. Fill in the rest and check it sounds right
steps.why: Read it aloud — does it make sense? ✓

learningGoal: How to tackle longer jumbled sentences without getting tangled up
A top tip: reading your rebuilt sentence aloud catches mistakes your eyes miss!

screens.title: Can you build this sentence?
screens.body:
Evie is tackling a trickier sentence puzzle.

Jumbled words: 'to walked every school they morning'

Longer sentences need the same method — find the subject, then the verb, then fill in the rest.

**What is the last word of the correct sentence?**

steps.text: Jumbled words: 'to walked every school they morning'
Subject → Verb → Rest

screens.title: Read it aloud!
screens.body:
The jumbled words make: **"They walked to school every morning."**

Once you've built a sentence, **read it aloud** (or in your head). Does it sound natural? If something feels wrong, try swapping words around.

steps.text: Find subject and verb first
steps.why: Who does what?

steps.text: Build the sentence: "They walked to school every morning."
steps.why: Add the remaining words where they sound natural

steps.text: Read aloud and check
steps.why: What is the last word of the correct sentence? → morning ✓

screens.title: Your turn — a new sentence!
screens.body:
Jumbled words: 'library quietly the read in children the'

Build the sentence, then answer:

interaction.question: What is the fourth word of the correct sentence?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The correct sentence is 'The children read quietly in the library.' The fourth word is 'quietly' — The (1st), children (2nd), read (3rd), quietly (4th). ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "quietly". The correct sentence is 'The children read quietly in the library.' The fourth word is 'quietly' — The (1st), children (2nd), read (3rd), quietly (4th). ✓

screens.title: Longer sentences? Same trick!
screens.body: Even longer sentences follow the same pattern. You've got this:

steps.text: 1. Subject first (who/what)
steps.why: The, A, She, He, They — often starts the sentence

steps.text: 2. Verb next (what they do)
steps.why: ran, played, walked, sang

steps.text: 3. Read it aloud — does it sound right?
steps.why: Your ear catches mistakes your eyes miss ✓

learningGoal: How to follow logical chains where one fact leads to another — like falling dominoes!
How to draw an arrow chain to make the logic crystal clear

screens.title: Can you follow the chain?
screens.body:
Aisha is working through a chain of logic.

A **syllogism** is a logic chain:
• If ALL X are Y...
• And Z is an X...
• Then Z must also be Y!

• All mammals are warm-blooded.
• A dog is a mammal.

**Is a dog warm-blooded?**

screens.title: Draw the arrow chain
screens.body: A syllogism (a logical chain where one fact leads to another) works like a chain of arrows. Each link passes the truth along:

screens.title: Your turn — a new chain!
screens.body:
Follow the arrow chain:

• All birds have feathers.
• A robin is a bird.

interaction.question: Does a robin have feathers?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! All birds have feathers. A robin is a bird. So a robin definitely has feathers. Follow the chain: robin → bird → feathers. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Yes, definitely". All birds have feathers. A robin is a bird. So a robin definitely has feathers. Follow the chain: robin → bird → feathers. ✓

screens.title: Syllogisms — follow the arrows!
screens.body: These logic chains are like dominoes — once you push the first one, they all fall in order:

steps.text: 1. Find the GENERAL rule (All X are Y)
steps.why: This is the starting link of the chain

steps.text: 2. Find the SPECIFIC fact (Z is an X)
steps.why: This connects to the rule

steps.text: 3. Follow the chain: Z → X → Y
steps.why: Each arrow passes the truth along ✓

learningGoal: Why 'not the tallest' does NOT mean 'the shortest' — this catches so many people!
How to read 'not', 'isn't' and 'neither' carefully instead of jumping to conclusions

screens.title: Does "not the tallest" mean "the shortest"?
screens.body:
Aisha is learning about negation traps.

• Kai is not the tallest of three friends.
• Freya is shorter than Kai.

**Is Kai definitely the shortest?**

"Not the tallest" is a **negation** — it tells you what something ISN'T, not what it IS. Be careful!

screens.title: Not tallest ≠ shortest!
screens.body:
We know: Kai is not the tallest of three friends. Freya is shorter than Kai.

"Not the tallest" just means **someone else is taller**. There could be people shorter too!

Always check: is there someone BELOW them in the order?

steps.text: "Not the tallest" means: at least one person is taller
steps.why: It rules out the TOP position only

steps.text: Check: is there anyone below? Freya is shorter than Kai.
steps.why: If yes, they're in the MIDDLE, not at the bottom

steps.text: Order: Tallest → Third friend → Kai → Freya → Shortest
steps.why: Is Kai definitely the shortest? → No — Kai is in the middle ✓

screens.title: Your turn — watch for the trap!
screens.body:
• Lily is not the fastest of three runners.
• Lily is faster than Max.

Remember: "not the tallest" does NOT automatically mean "the shortest"!

interaction.question: Is Lily the slowest?
options: No — Lily is in the middle
interaction.correctAnswer: No — Lily is in the middle
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Lily is not the fastest, but she IS faster than Max. So Lily is in the MIDDLE. 'Not the fastest' doesn't mean 'the slowest'! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "No — Lily is in the middle". Lily is not the fastest, but she IS faster than Max. So Lily is in the MIDDLE. 'Not the fastest' doesn't mean 'the slowest'! ✓

screens.title: Negation traps — you're too smart for those!
screens.body: Now you know the trick, you'll never fall for "not = opposite" again:

steps.text: "Not the tallest" just means someone is taller
steps.why: It does NOT mean shortest

steps.text: "Not first" just means someone finished before them
steps.why: It does NOT mean last

steps.text: Always draw the FULL order to find their actual position
steps.why: Use all the clues, not just the negation ✓

learningGoal: How to spot when someone else has fallen for the negation trap
Why drawing the full order is your best protection against this mistake

screens.title: Did they fall for the negation trap?
screens.body:
Evie is checking a friend's logic homework.

• Toby is not the fastest of three runners.
• Toby is faster than Grace.

**Who is the slowest?**

Someone answered **"Toby"** — because 'not the fastest' means he must be slowest.

Is that right?

screens.title: Draw the order to avoid the trap!
screens.body:
Toby is faster than Grace, so Grace is slowest! 'Not the fastest' just means he's not in first place.

The best way to avoid negation traps is to **draw the full order**. Then you can SEE where everyone belongs.

steps.text: Clue: Toby is not the fastest of three runners.
steps.why: This tells us their position is NOT at the top

steps.text: Clue: Toby is faster than Grace.
steps.why: This tells us someone is BELOW them

steps.text: Draw: Fastest → Third runner → Toby → Grace → Slowest
steps.why: Toby is faster than Grace, so Grace is slowest! 'Not the fastest' just means he's not in first place. ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Don't fall for the trap! Draw the order:

• Mia is not the tallest of three girls.
• Mia is taller than Poppy.

interaction.question: Who is the shortest?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Mia isn't the tallest, but she IS taller than Poppy. So Poppy is the shortest. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "Poppy". Mia isn't the tallest, but she IS taller than Poppy. So Poppy is the shortest. ✓

screens.title: Negation trap? Not a chance!
screens.body: You've mastered this. Every time you see "not" or "isn't", just remember:

steps.text: 1. DON'T assume the opposite extreme
steps.why: Not tallest ≠ shortest, not first ≠ last

steps.text: 2. Draw the full order using ALL clues
steps.why: The diagram shows you their actual position

steps.text: 3. There are usually positions in between!
steps.why: With 3+ people, the middle exists ✓

learningGoal: You'll learn how to spot when a word is playing tricks — having more than one meaning hiding behind the same spelling!
You'll master using the sentence context to choose the right meaning every time

screens.title: Which "bank" is it?
screens.body:
Daisy reads this sentence in her comprehension passage:

**"We sat on the bank of the river and watched the ducks."**

Did you know? The word **"bank"** has more than one meaning! Words like this are called **homonyms** — same spelling, completely different meanings. English is full of them!

**Meaning 1:** a place where you keep money
**Meaning 2:** the side of a river

Which meaning is being used here? The sentence holds the answer!

visualProps.text: We sat on the bank of the river and watched the ducks.
visualProps.label: Target word: "bank"

screens.title: The sentence always gives it away!
screens.body: Is **"bank"** being used to mean "a place where you keep money" or "the side of a river"? Here's the thing: when a word has multiple meanings, the **context** (the words around it) always tells you which meaning is being used. Look for **clue words** that match one meaning but not the other — they're your best friends here!

steps.text: Read: "We sat on the bank of the river and watched the ducks."
steps.why: Focus on the word "bank"

steps.text: Find the clue words: "river, sat on, watched the ducks"
steps.why: These words narrow it down

steps.text: Do the clues match "a place where you keep money"?
steps.why: Test the first meaning against the clues

steps.text: "bank" here means "the side of a river" ✓
steps.why: The clues 'river' and 'watched the ducks' tell us this bank is the sloping ground beside water, not a building for money

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Now you try — which meaning of **"bank"** is being used here? Hunt for those clue words!

**"Mum went to the bank to pay in a cheque this morning."**

interaction.question: Which meaning of "bank" is used here?
options: a place where you keep money
interaction.correctAnswer: a place where you keep money
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The clues 'pay in' and 'cheque' tell us this bank is a financial building where you manage money, not the side of a river. The clue words gave it away! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — this one's tricky! The clues 'pay in' and 'cheque' tell us this bank is a financial building where you manage money, not the side of a river. Look at the words around the homonym next time.

visualProps.text: Mum went to the bank to pay in a cheque this morning.
visualProps.label: Find the answer for "bank":

screens.title: Homonyms — same word, different meaning!
screens.body: You've got this! Here's how to crack homonyms every time they pop up in the exam:

steps.text: Step 1: Spot that the word has more than one meaning
steps.why: Common homonyms: bank, bat, light, ring, match, fair — they come up loads!

steps.text: Step 2: Read the whole sentence carefully
steps.why: The context always gives the game away

steps.text: Step 3: Find clue words that match ONE meaning
steps.why: River → bank of a river, not a money bank — the clues point the way

steps.text: Step 4: Pick the meaning that fits the clues
steps.why: If all the clues match, you've cracked it! ✓

learningGoal: You'll discover that the same word can actually be used as different word classes (noun, verb, adjective) — words are shapeshifters!
You'll get really good at matching the right definition to the right context

screens.title: "match" strikes again!
screens.body:
Here's a fun one! Grace discovers that the word **"match"** has completely different meanings depending on how it's used:

**Sentence 1:** "Evie struck a match to light the birthday candles." — here it means **a small stick used to start a fire**

**Sentence 2:** "Does this hat match my scarf?" — here it means something totally different!

Homonyms are words that are **spelled the same** but have **different meanings**. The sentence around the word is always the key to cracking which one it is.

screens.title: Same spelling, different meaning!
screens.body: Remember **"match"** from the previous screen? It's a homonym (a word spelled the same but with different meanings). Here's the pro tip: **ignore** what you think the word means and focus on what the **sentence** tells you. The words around it always give the answer away — you just have to look!

steps.text: Read: "Does this hat match my scarf?"
steps.why: What is "match" doing in this sentence?

steps.text: It can't mean "a small stick used to start a fire"
steps.why: That doesn't fit this sentence at all

steps.text: It can't mean "a sports game" either
steps.why: That's another meaning but still doesn't fit here

steps.text: "match" here means "to go well together" ✓
steps.why: This is the only meaning that makes the sentence work

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
You've got this! What does **"match"** mean in this sentence? Look at the context!

**"We watched an exciting match between the two school football teams."**

interaction.question: What does "match" mean in this sentence?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Watched' and 'between two teams' tell us this match is a sports game — a competitive event, not about colours or fire. You read the context perfectly! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — but homonyms are properly tricky! 'Watched' and 'between two teams' tell us this match is a sports game — a competitive event, not about colours or fire. The context clues will get you there next time.

visualProps.text: We watched an exciting match between the two school football teams.
visualProps.label: Find the meaning of "match":

screens.title: Homonyms — don't be tricked!
screens.body: In the exam, homonyms pop up everywhere — but now you know how to handle them like a pro:

steps.text: Step 1: Notice that the word has multiple meanings
steps.why: If you know two meanings, the exam could use either one — stay alert!

steps.text: Step 2: Read the WHOLE sentence, not just the word
steps.why: The sentence always tells you which meaning is right

steps.text: Step 3: Try each meaning in the sentence
steps.why: Only one will make sense — the others will sound completely off

steps.text: Step 4: Pick the meaning that fits perfectly
steps.why: Context is king — and now you rule it! ✓
